posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: I don’t recall the exact time line but my recollection is Bob Mondavi did a pretty good job of diluting his brand long before Constellation took over completely in 2004.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: Certainly Constellation has some rights to the name - they paid a big number for Mondavi and deserve the right to preserve the name. Not withstanding that, it is a creek which is located in a place. I am very confused as to how naming a creek To-Kalon damages their brand - especially considering Macdonald sold their fruit to Mondavi for their Reserve Wines for decades.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
JBierly:

don’t recall the exact time line but my recollection is Bob Mondavi did a pretty good job of diluting his brand long before Constellation took over completely in 2004.

Read the House of Mondavi some time ago, so don’t recall the exact details. But my recollection was that when it was sold it was not a supermarket brand.

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked:
JBierly:

how naming a creek To-Kalon damages their brand

The underlying issue is that if becomes a geographic name, then the To Kalon trademark could lose exclusivity, be watered down, or potentially voided.

Think Macdonald “To Kalon Creek Vineyard” Cabernet.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: Makes sense except it would only be applicable to the geography which is almost wholly owned by Constellation. I guess MacDonald and Detert could be affected. Who else?

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: Woodbridge was started in 1979.

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked: Beckstoffer, though their settlement may make it moot. Either way, even if the change doesn’t really matter today, who knows what tomorrow might bring.

posts.name: Scott G r u n 3 r
posts.cooked: Seems ludicrous to me that someone can trademark a vineyard name that has been around and in common use for 150 years and force other current legal owners of the vineyard to not use it.
posts.display_username: Scott G r u n 3 r

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: I am not certain they couldn’t do that now but I think that would be counter to their current strategy of selling parts of their portfolio that is lower end.

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked: You’re missing the point. That example is just for impact. They could use it for a high-end wine also from another part of Napa or Sonoma or anywhere if it is a brand. As a brand, it is much more valuable to them than if it is attached to a specific site.

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked:
I think it’s already been established that it is currently a brand name. They currently can sell your example, though it’s clear that would be folly.

Then again, the Mondavi name has been so watered down, its not out of the realm of possibility.

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked: And that is entirely the point! Right now it is a brand. If it becomes a place also there can be a challenge to it being a brand either directly or indirectly

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked: The vote was delayed. That article seems to have stirred up some additional interest from people in the area so they’re taking a closer look again at it before voting

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked:
I get the point. Nothing is stopping them right now from doing that considering they own the trademark. I am of the opinion that irrespective of the judgement over the creek that they can put ToKalon on any bottle they choose. And the ToKalon Vineyard Company at least has the appearance of sourcing grapes only from the To Kalon Vineyard.

“To Kalon Vineyard Company is dedicated to crafting unique expressions of Cabernet Sauvignon from this storied place.” Maybe that isn’t truthful - I guess crafting doesn’t necessarily mean the grapes are grown in the To Kalon Vineyard. Not withstanding that the bios on the wines are pretty explicit that the vineyard is To Kalon and at least for H.W.C it “is sourced from one block of vines planted to a heritage clone of Cabernet Sauvignon that produces impossibly small clusters of tiny berries yielding wines with remarkable energy and concentration.”

I just don’t see why Constellation would want to dilute the To Kalon name in the same way that Mondavi diluted his brand with Woodbridge (1979) and Private Selection (1994) brands but maybe you’re right - To Kalon White Zin for $19.99 - a vertitable bargain.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: I somehow doubt that a ruling on the Creek in and of itself would apply to the trademark as a whole. Someone (like Beckstoffer) would then have to litigate that only wines from grapes grown in To Kalon can actually have the name on the bottle. AFAIK, at present Constellation basically allows him to use their trademark. I certainly might be missing something…

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked:
JBierly:

ToKalon Vineyard Company at least has the appearance of sourcing grapes only from the To Kalon Vineyard

It’s also owned by Constellation, so just seems to be another brand extension.

posts.name: Zach Goldstein
posts.cooked:
It wouldn’t apply to the trademark at all. The idea is that it would devalue it while increasing the value of Macdonald (in theory). Constellation being the sole trademark holder is clearly super important to them that they’re willing to go this far over it. I guarantee some intellectual property lawyers are pushing them this way and they are seeing the Macdonalds as a massive thorn in their side

Whole thing is so silly after all, as any wine lover already knows Macdonald is one of the best wines in the valley and it’s not like them adding the name to their bottles would make an already fully allocated wine more desirable

posts.name: Chris Weishaar
posts.cooked:
JBierly:

that Mondavi diluted his brand with Woodbridge (1979) and Private Selection (1994) brands

You say this, but I’m not buying it at all. Woodbridge and Private Selection are lines within Mondavi’s portfolio, but in no way do I associate their existence as a dilution of other Mondavi labels. Those lines had no impact on the Reserve Cabernets of the ‘90s, wines which are still drinking beautifully today.

Those lines were merely diversification, which is a wise thing for many businesses.

posts.name: Lee Braem
posts.cooked: The To Kalon trademark for a brand is a travesty. It goes against the whole AVA concept - based on uniqueness of geography, climate, etc.

posts.name: Jeff M 0 L l
posts.cooked: Would the loss of the To Kalon trademark endanger Chappellet’s Pritchard Hill trademark?

posts.name: Marshall Newman
posts.cooked: There have been a handful of these fights in Napa Valley over the years. A “Calistoga” label trademark stalled the Calistoga AVA for a decade. A “Tulocay” trademark derailed an AVA petition for the name completely, resulting in the eventual AVA being called “Coombsville.”

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked:
The Mondavi strategy is basically a business case for what brand dilution actually is. Cadillac Cimarron ( a rebranded Chevrolet Cavalier) didn’t have any impact on its Seville but it was a terrible thing for the Cadillac Brand. Ralph Lauren did so much licensing to crappy lines (like Mondavi did with Woodbridge) that it eroded his brand but it didn’t have any impact on its Purple Label (that’s the high end label that is made in Italy). According to a NYT article published in 2003, Mondavi said, “We’ve got to get our image back, and that’s going to take time.”

AI Summary: Robert Mondavi expressed regret and criticized his sons for their business strategy which emphasized the inexpensive Woodbridge and Coastal lines over their premium wines. He felt this focus had negatively impacted the company’s image and its association with fine wine.

Brand dilution isn’t about the original brand being worse - it’s about the diversification to lesser brands damaging the overall value of the brand - something they clearly did. Imagine if Harlan suddenly came out with their Coastal Brand?

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked:
Ziplock by Louis Vuitton

I mean they are both bags, right?

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: And the LVZiplock didn’t effect the value of my real LV handbag which is still holding up almost as well as my Chinese fake version I bought on 34th Street.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: What is somewhat perplexing about all this is that Constellation doesn’t actually seem to have any aspirations to dilute the “brand value” To Kalon. All the branding materials of To Kalon Wine Company and the use of “To Kalon Vineyard” on the Mondavi labels leans into the notion of terroir and To Kalon being a special place—indeed, to quote their own materials, a “Grand Cru vineyard”. Yet Constellation are simultaneously opposing the name of the creek that runs through this same vineyard (and which indeed corresponds with the watercourse that actually created the alluvial fan that corresponds pretty precisely with the historical footprint of the To Kalon vineyard). Bizarre, and regrettable.

posts.name: Jack Bierly
posts.cooked: Agree totally. I have to think it is about control and “what ifs?” which is the way attorneys go through life. Mondavi sold the name to Constellation - no idea what the brand Mondavi or ToKalon is on their books but it has to be something pretty consequential. Constellation was willing to let MacDonald use To Kalon on their wine - akin to what they have done with Beckstoffer. They know the consequences of that. I think it is about the unknown. If indeed a creek is named To Kalon then what is the next thing? And how will it affect a balance sheet item that they own. When I first visited Mondavi in 1981 they were The Winery. Much has changed since then but one thing really hasn’t - To Kalon still is The Vineyard. From a strategy point of view it is one thing that gives them a sustainable competitive advantage. If I were running the show I would do everything possible to work that angle, maintain that image, and defend that brand - even moreso than the Mondavi brand which is pretty clear that even Bob thought had been damaged prior to his death. And although they have had ongoing lawsuits with Beckstoffer, if anything all the wineries that Beckstoffer has sourced fruit to has made the brand ToKalon much bigger. Not withstanding the fact that Mondavi damaged their brand by coming up with lesser wines that bore their name, the one thing that was very smart was that they did trademark To-Kalon. I think the owners will die on that hill defending that brand. Buying Schrader was a step in the right direction - just how many 100 point bottles does Schrader have from To-Kalon Fruit. Coming out with To Kalon Vineyard Company is another step in that direction - moving more towards To Kalon. It will be interesting to visit the renovated winery (opens next year?). I am sure the marketing materials will be interesting. My guess is there will be a lot of focus on To-Kalon.
